```csv
furnace_id,furnace_capacity
F1,50
F2,40

```

### data/grades_lines.csv
```csv
grade_id,line_id,profit
G1,L1,300
G1,L2,280
G2,L1,250
G2,L2,240

```

### data/lines_furnaces.csv
```csv
line_id,furnace_id,line_furnace_assign
L1,F1,1
L1,F2,0
L2,F1,0
L2,F2,1

```

### data/scrap_quality.csv
```csv
scrap_id,quality_attr,scrap_composition
S1,Q1,0.4
S1,Q2,0.6
S2,Q1,0.7
S2,Q2,0.3

```

### data/additive_quality.csv
```csv
additive_id,quality_attr,additive_composition
A1,Q1,0.9
A1,Q2,0.1
A2,Q1,0.2
A2,Q2,0.8

```

### data/grades_quality.csv
```csv
grade_id,quality_attr,grade_min_quality,grade_max_quality
G1,Q1,0.5,0.8
G1,Q2,0.2,0.5
G2,Q1,0.3,0.6
G2,Q2,0.4,0.7

```
answer:
import gurobipy as gp
from gurobipy import GRB
import json
import os

with open("instance.json") as f:
    instance = json.load(f)
bigM = instance["bigM"]

import pandas as pd

grades_df = pd.read_csv("data/grades.csv")
lines_df = pd.read_csv("data/lines.csv")
scrap_df = pd.read_csv("data/scrap.csv")
additives_df = pd.read_csv("data/additives.csv")
furnaces_df = pd.read_csv("data/furnaces.csv")
grades_lines_df = pd.read_csv("data/grades_lines.csv")
lines_furnaces_df = pd.read_csv("data/lines_furnaces.csv")
scrap_quality_df = pd.read_csv("data/scrap_quality.csv")
additive_quality_df = pd.read_csv("data/additive_quality.csv")
grades_quality_df = pd.read_csv("data/grades_quality.csv")

grades = grades_df["grade_id"].tolist()
lines = lines_df["line_id"].tolist()
scraps = scrap_df["scrap_id"].tolist()
additives = additives_df["additive_id"].tolist()
furnaces = furnaces_df["furnace_id"].tolist()
quality_attrs = scrap_quality_df["quality_attr"].unique().tolist()

grade_demand = dict(zip(grades_df["grade_id"], grades_df["grade_demand"]))
setup_cost = dict(zip(lines_df["line_id"], lines_df["setup_cost"]))
line_min_batches = dict(zip(lines_df["line_id"], lines_df["line_min_batches"]))
line_max_batches = dict(zip(lines_df["line_id"], lines_df["line_max_batches"]))
purchase_cost = dict(zip(scrap_df["scrap_id"], scrap_df["purchase_cost"]))
scrap_availability = dict(zip(scrap_df["scrap_id"], scrap_df["scrap_availability"]))
additive_cost = dict(zip(additives_df["additive_id"], additives_df["additive_cost"]))
additive_availability = dict(zip(additives_df["additive_id"], additives_df["additive_availability"]))
furnace_capacity = dict(zip(furnaces_df["furnace_id"], furnaces_df["furnace_capacity"]))

profit = {}
for _, row in grades_lines_df.iterrows():
    profit[(row["grade_id"], row["line_id"])] = row["profit"]

line_furnace_assign = {}
for _, row in lines_furnaces_df.iterrows():
    line_furnace_assign[(row["line_id"], row["furnace_id"])] = row["line_furnace_assign"]

scrap_composition = {}
for _, row in scrap_quality_df.iterrows():
    scrap_composition[(row["scrap_id"], row["quality_attr"])] = row["scrap_composition"]

additive_composition = {}
for _, row in additive_quality_df.iterrows():
    additive_composition[(row["additive_id"], row["quality_attr"])] = row["additive_composition"]

grade_min_quality = {}
grade_max_quality = {}
for _, row in grades_quality_df.iterrows():
    grade_min_quality[(row["grade_id"], row["quality_attr"])] = row["grade_min_quality"]
    grade_max_quality[(row["grade_id"], row["quality_attr"])] = row["grade_max_quality"]

model = gp.Model("steel_foundry")

x = model.addVars(grades, lines, vtype=GRB.INTEGER, lb=0, name="x")
y = model.addVars(lines, vtype=GRB.BINARY, name="y")
z = model.addVars(scraps, vtype=GRB.INTEGER, lb=0, name="z")
w = model.addVars(additives, vtype=GRB.INTEGER, lb=0, name="w")
v = model.addVars(furnaces, vtype=GRB.BINARY, name="v")

model.setObjective(
    gp.quicksum(profit[g, l] * x[g, l] for g in grades for l in lines)
    - gp.quicksum(setup_cost[l] * y[l] for l in lines)
    - gp.quicksum(purchase_cost[s] * z[s] for s in scraps),
    GRB.MAXIMIZE
)

for g in grades:
    model.addConstr(gp.quicksum(x[g, l] for l in lines) >= grade_demand[g], name=f"demand_satisfaction_{g}")

for l in lines:
    model.addConstr(gp.quicksum(x[g, l] for g in grades) >= line_min_batches[l] * y[l], name=f"line_min_batch_{l}")
    model.addConstr(gp.quicksum(x[g, l] for g in grades) <= line_max_batches[l] * y[l], name=f"line_max_batch_{l}")
    model.addConstr(gp.quicksum(x[g, l] for g in grades) <= bigM * y[l], name=f"setup_linking_{l}")

for s in scraps:
    model.addConstr(z[s] <= scrap_availability[s], name=f"scrap_availability_{s}")

for a in additives:
    model.addConstr(w[a] <= additive_availability[a], name=f"additive_availability_{a}")

for f in furnaces:
    model.addConstr(
        gp.quicksum(line_furnace_assign[l, f] * gp.quicksum(x[g, l] for g in grades) for l in lines)
        <= furnace_capacity[f] * v[f],
        name=f"furnace_capacity_{f}"
    )
    model.addConstr(
        gp.quicksum(line_furnace_assign[l, f] * y[l] for l in lines) <= bigM * v[f],
        name=f"furnace_activation_{f}"
    )

for l in lines:
    model.addConstr(
        gp.quicksum(line_furnace_assign[l, f] * v[f] for f in furnaces) == y[l],
        name=f"line_furnace_exclusive_{l}"
    )

for l in lines:
    model.addConstr(
        gp.quicksum(x[g, l] for g in grades)
        <= gp.quicksum(z[s] for s in scraps) + gp.quicksum(w[a] for a in additives),
        name=f"material_balance_{l}"
    )

for g in grades:
    for q in quality_attrs:
        model.addConstr(
            gp.quicksum(scrap_composition[s, q] * z[s] for s in scraps)
            + gp.quicksum(additive_composition[a, q] * w[a] for a in additives)
            >= grade_min_quality[g, q] * gp.quicksum(x[g, l] for l in lines),
            name=f"quality_lower_{g}_{q}"
        )
        model.addConstr(
            gp.quicksum(scrap_composition[s, q] * z[s] for s in scraps)
            + gp.quicksum(additive_composition[a, q] * w[a] for a in additives)
            <= grade_max_quality[g, q] * gp.quicksum(x[g, l] for l in lines),
            name=f"quality_upper_{g}_{q}"
        )

os.makedirs("output", exist_ok=True)
model.write("output/model.lp")

problem:
A paint factory blends four base pigments (P1, P2, P3, P4) to produce three custom colors (C1, C2, C3). Each color has a minimum and maximum allowed fraction of a critical additive. The factory must meet the demand for each color while minimizing total pigment cost, subject to pigment availability and blending ratio constraints.

**Pigment Data**

| Pigment | Cost per liter | Additive fraction | Availability (liters) |
|---------|----------------|-------------------|----------------------|
| P1      | 5              | 0.10             | 200                  |
| P2      | 7              | 0.25             | 150                  |
| P3      | 4              | 0.05             | 300                  |
| P4      | 6              | 0.30             | 180                  |

**Color Requirements**

- Color C1: demand = 100 liters, additive fraction must be between 0.12 and 0.20.
- Color C2: demand = 80 liters, additive fraction must be between 0.15 and 0.28.
- Color C3: demand = 120 liters, additive fraction must be between 0.08 and 0.18.

**Decision Variables**

Let x_{c,p} be the liters of pigment p used in color c (continuous, nonnegative).

**Objective**

Minimize total cost: sum over colors c and pigments p of (cost of pigment p) * x_{c,p}.

**Constraints**

1. Demand satisfaction: For each color c, the total amount of all pigments used must be at least the demand of that color.
2. Minimum additive ratio: For each color c, the total additive content (sum over pigments p of additive fraction of p * x_{c,p}) must be at least the minimum additive fraction for that color times the total amount of all pigments used in that color.
3. Maximum additive ratio: For each color c, the total additive content must be at most the maximum additive fraction for that color times the total amount of all pigments used in that color.
4. Pigment supply: For each pigment p, the total amount used across all colors must not exceed the availability of that pigment.
answer:
<think>
The problem involves blending four pigments (P1–P4) into three colors (C1–C3). Each color has a demand, a minimum and maximum additive fraction. Pigments have cost, additive fraction, and availability. The goal is to minimize total cost while meeting demand and additive ratio constraints.

**Decision variables**: x_{c,p} = liters of pigment p used in color c, continuous and nonnegative.

**Objective**: Minimize sum_{c,p} cost_p * x_{c,p}.

**Constraints**:
1. Demand: sum_p x_{c,p} >= demand_c for each color c.
2. Min additive: sum_p additive_p * x_{c,p} >= min_ratio_c * sum_p x_{c,p}.
3. Max additive: sum_p additive_p * x_{c,p} <= max_ratio_c * sum_p x_{c,p}.
4. Pigment supply: sum_c x_{c,p} <= availability_p for each pigment p.

We implement this in Gurobi.
</think>

<model>
**Sets**
- Colors = {C1, C2, C3}
- Pigments = {P1, P2, P3, P4}

**Parameters**
- cost_p: cost per liter of pigment p
- additive_p: fraction of critical additive in pigment p
- availability_p: maximum liters of pigment p available
- demand_c: minimum liters of color c required
- min_additive_ratio_c: minimum additive fraction for color c
- max_additive_ratio_c: maximum additive fraction for color c

**Decision Variables**
- x_{c,p} >= 0: liters of pigment p used in color c

**Objective**
Minimize total cost:
\[ \sum_{c \in Colors} \sum_{p \in Pigments} \text{cost}_p \cdot x_{c,p} \]

**Constraints**
1. Demand satisfaction: \[ \sum_{p \in Pigments} x_{c,p} \geq \text{demand}_c \quad \forall c \in Colors \]
2. Minimum additive ratio: \[ \sum_{p \in Pigments} \text{additive}_p \cdot x_{c,p} \geq \text{min\_additive\_ratio}_c \cdot \sum_{p \in Pigments} x_{c,p} \quad \forall c \in Colors \]
3. Maximum additive ratio: \[ \sum_{p \in Pigments} \text{additive}_p \cdot x_{c,p} \leq \text{max\_additive\_ratio}_c \cdot \sum_{p \in Pigments} x_{c,p} \quad \forall c \in Colors \]
4. Pigment supply: \[ \sum_{c \in Colors} x_{c,p} \leq \text{availability}_p \quad \forall p \in Pigments \]
</model>

<python>
import gurobipy as gp
from gurobipy import GRB

# Data
colors = ['C1', 'C2', 'C3']
pigments = ['P1', 'P2', 'P3', 'P4']

cost = {'P1': 5, 'P2': 7, 'P3': 4, 'P4': 6}
additive = {'P1': 0.10, 'P2': 0.25, 'P3': 0.05, 'P4': 0.30}
availability = {'P1': 200, 'P2': 150, 'P3': 300, 'P4': 180}

demand = {'C1': 100, 'C2': 80, 'C3': 120}
min_additive_ratio = {'C1': 0.12, 'C2': 0.15, 'C3': 0.08}
max_additive_ratio = {'C1': 0.20, 'C2': 0.28, 'C3': 0.18}

# Model
model = gp.Model("paint_blending")

# Decision variables
x = model.addVars(colors, pigments, name="x", lb=0)

# Objective: minimize total cost
model.setObjective(gp.quicksum(cost[p] * x[c, p] for c in colors for p in pigments), GRB.MINIMIZE)

# Constraints
# 1. Demand satisfaction
for c in colors:
    model.addConstr(gp.quicksum(x[c, p] for p in pigments) >= demand[c], name=f"demand_{c}")

# 2. Minimum additive ratio
for c in colors:
    model.addConstr(gp.quicksum(additive[p] * x[c, p] for p in pigments) >= min_additive_ratio[c] * gp.quicksum(x[c, p] for p in pigments), name=f"min_add_{c}")

# 3. Maximum additive ratio
for c in colors:
    model.addConstr(gp.quicksum(additive[p] * x[c, p] for p in pigments) <= max_additive_ratio[c] * gp.quicksum(x[c, p] for p in pigments), name=f"max_add_{c}")

# 4. Pigment supply
for p in pigments:
    model.addConstr(gp.quicksum(x[c, p] for c in colors) <= availability[p], name=f"supply_{p}")

# Optimize
model.optimize()

if model.status == GRB.OPTIMAL:
    print(f"Optimal total cost: {model.objVal}")
else:
    print("No optimal solution found.")
</python>

problem:
## Problem Statement

A marketing firm is planning a campaign across multiple channels to maximize total engagement. The firm has a fixed budget and must decide how much to allocate to each channel. Each channel has a cost per unit allocation, a maximum allowable spend, and a base engagement rate per unit allocation. Additionally, there are cross-channel synergy effects: for each pair of distinct channels, a synergy coefficient multiplies the sum of allocations in that pair, adding to the total engagement. The total cost must not exceed the budget.

## Background

Marketing campaigns often involve multiple channels such as social media, TV, and print. The engagement generated by each channel depends on the amount spent, and synergies between channels can amplify the overall effect. The firm needs to allocate its budget optimally to maximize the total engagement score.

## Problem Description

Given a set of marketing channels, each with a cost per unit allocation, a base engagement per unit allocation, and a maximum allowable spend, and given a total budget and pairwise synergy coefficients, determine the allocation amounts for each channel that maximize the total engagement score, subject to the budget constraint and the maximum spend limits.

## Parameters

The problem data are provided in the file `data.json`. The file contains the following fields:

- `budget`: a float, the total campaign budget.
- `cost`: a list of floats, where `cost[c]` is the cost per unit allocation for channel `c`.
- `base_engagement`: a list of floats, where `base_engagement[c]` is the base engagement per unit allocation for channel `c`.
- `max_spend`: a list of floats, where `max_spend[c]` is the maximum allowable allocation for channel `c`.
- `synergy`: a list of lists of floats, where `synergy[c1][c2]` is the synergy coefficient for the unordered pair of channels `c1` and `c2` (symmetric, zero on diagonal and for `c1 >= c2`).

## Task

Formulate the optimization model using the provided data. Construct a mathematical model that maximizes the total engagement score, subject to the budget constraint and the maximum spend constraints.

The following structured data files are provided:

### data.json
```json
{
  "budget": 1000.0,
  "cost": [
    10.0,
    15.0,
    20.0
  ],
  "base_engagement": [
    5.0,
    8.0,
    12.0
  ],
  "max_spend": [
    50.0,
    40.0,
    30.0
  ],
  "synergy": [
    [
      0.0,
      0.3,
      0.2
    ],
    [
      0.0,
      0.0,
      0.4
    ],
    [
      0.0,
      0.0,
      0.0
    ]
  ]
}
```
answer:
import json
import gurobipy as gp
from gurobipy import GRB

with open('data.json', 'r') as f:
    data = json.load(f)

budget = data['budget']
cost = data['cost']
base_engagement = data['base_engagement']
max_spend = data['max_spend']
synergy = data['synergy']

num_channels = len(cost)
channels = range(num_channels)

model = gp.Model('marketing_campaign')

x = model.addVars(channels, lb=0, ub=max_spend, vtype=GRB.CONTINUOUS, name='x')

obj = gp.QuadExpr()
for c in channels:
    obj += base_engagement[c] * x[c]
for c1 in channels:
    for c2 in channels:
        if c1 < c2:
            obj += synergy[c1][c2] * (x[c1] + x[c2])

model.setObjective(obj, GRB.MAXIMIZE)

model.addConstr(gp.quicksum(cost[c] * x[c] for c in channels) <= budget, name='budget_constraint')

model.write('model.lp')

problem:
A telecommunications company operates a network of 3 relay stations and 4 destination nodes. Each relay station has a limited data supply (in gigabytes), and each destination node has a known data demand (in gigabytes). The profit per gigabyte transmitted from each relay to each destination is given in the table below. The company must decide how many gigabytes to transmit on each link to maximize total profit, subject to the supply and demand constraints.

| Relay | Destination | Profit per GB |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | 1 | 3.2 |
| 1 | 2 | 2.8 |
| 1 | 3 | 4.1 |
| 1 | 4 | 1.9 |
| 2 | 1 | 2.5 |
| 2 | 2 | 3.0 |
| 2 | 3 | 3.6 |
| 2 | 4 | 2.2 |
| 3 | 1 | 4.0 |
| 3 | 2 | 3.4 |
| 3 | 3 | 2.9 |
| 3 | 4 | 3.8 |

Data supply (GB) at each relay: Relay 1 = 50, Relay 2 = 60, Relay 3 = 45.
Data demand (GB) at each destination: Destination 1 = 40, Destination 2 = 30, Destination 3 = 35, Destination 4 = 25.
answer:
<think>
This is a classic network flow problem. We have 3 relay stations (supply nodes) and 4 destination nodes (demand nodes). The profit per GB is given for each relay-destination pair. The goal is to maximize total profit by deciding how many gigabytes to transmit on each link, subject to supply limits at relays and demand limits at destinations.

We define:
- Sets: RELAYS = {1,2,3}, DESTINATIONS = {1,2,3,4}
- Parameters: supply_r (GB available at relay r), demand_d (GB required at destination d), profit_{r,d} (profit per GB from relay r to destination d)
- Decision variables: x_{r,d} >= 0, continuous, representing GB transmitted from relay r to destination d
- Objective: maximize sum_{r,d} profit_{r,d} * x_{r,d}
- Constraints:
  1. Supply limit: for each relay r, sum_d x_{r,d} <= supply_r
  2. Demand fulfillment: for each destination d, sum_r x_{r,d} <= demand_d

This is a linear program that can be solved with Gurobi.
</think>

<model>
**Sets**
- R = {1, 2, 3} : set of relay stations
- D = {1, 2, 3, 4} : set of destination nodes

**Parameters**
- supply_r : data supply (GB) at relay r ∈ R
- demand_d : data demand (GB) at destination d ∈ D
- profit_{r,d} : profit per GB transmitted from relay r to destination d

**Decision Variables**
- x_{r,d} ≥ 0, continuous : gigabytes transmitted from relay r to destination d

**Objective**
Maximize total profit:
∑_{r∈R} ∑_{d∈D} profit_{r,d} * x_{r,d}

**Constraints**
1. Supply limit at each relay:
   ∑_{d∈D} x_{r,d} ≤ supply_r, ∀ r ∈ R
2. Demand fulfillment at each destination:
   ∑_{r∈R} x_{r,d} ≤ demand_d, ∀ d ∈ D
</model>

<python>
import gurobipy as gp
from gurobipy import GRB

# Data
relays = [1, 2, 3]
destinations = [1, 2, 3, 4]

supply = {1: 50, 2: 60, 3: 45}
demand = {1: 40, 2: 30, 3: 35, 4: 25}

profit = {
    (1, 1): 3.2, (1, 2): 2.8, (1, 3): 4.1, (1, 4): 1.9,
    (2, 1): 2.5, (2, 2): 3.0, (2, 3): 3.6, (2, 4): 2.2,
    (3, 1): 4.0, (3, 2): 3.4, (3, 3): 2.9, (3, 4): 3.8
}

# Create model
model = gp.Model("telecom_profit")

# Decision variables
x = model.addVars(relays, destinations, vtype=GRB.CONTINUOUS, lb=0, name="x")

# Objective: maximize total profit
model.setObjective(
    gp.quicksum(profit[r, d] * x[r, d] for r in relays for d in destinations),
    GRB.MAXIMIZE
)

# Supply constraints
for r in relays:
    model.addConstr(
        gp.quicksum(x[r, d] for d in destinations) <= supply[r],
        name=f"supply_limit_{r}"
    )

# Demand constraints
for d in destinations:
    model.addConstr(
        gp.quicksum(x[r, d] for r in relays) <= demand[d],
        name=f"demand_fulfill_{d}"
    )

# Optimize
model.optimize()

if model.status == GRB.OPTIMAL:
    print(f"Optimal total profit: {model.objVal}")
else:
    print("No optimal solution found.")
</python>

problem:
## Problem Statement

A shipping company operates a network of ports and transshipment hubs. The company must route a set of container shipments through the network using a set of vessel services. Each service can be activated at a fixed cost and provides a sequence of directed arcs (port-to-port or port-to-hub) with a transit time. Each container has an origin port, a destination port, a volume, a delivery deadline, and a required hub capacity class. The objective is to minimize the total cost, which includes service activation costs, hub handling costs, and late-delivery penalties.

## Background

The network consists of port nodes and hub nodes. Vessel services operate on predefined directed arcs between nodes. Containers can be routed over multiple services and may transship through hubs. Each hub has capacity limits per capacity class and charges a handling cost per container volume. If a container arrives after its deadline, a penalty is incurred per time unit late. Unrouted container volume is allowed but discouraged by the objective.

## Problem Description

Given the sets and parameters below, formulate a mixed-integer linear programming model that decides:
- Which vessel services to activate (binary variables).
- Which service arcs each container uses (binary variables).
- How many times each container transships through each hub (integer variables).
- The late time for each container (integer variables).
- The unrouted volume for each container (integer variables).

The model must respect service capacity, flow conservation at each node for each container, hub capacity per class, and the relationship between transit time and late time. The objective is to minimize the sum of service activation costs, hub handling costs, and late penalties.

## Parameters

The instance data are provided in the following files:
- **instance.json**: Contains the counts of ports, hubs, services, containers, and hub capacity classes.
- **services.csv**: For each service, its ID, activation cost, and container capacity.
- **service_arcs.csv**: For each service and each arc in its route, the arc ID, origin node, destination node, and transit time.
- **containers.csv**: For each container, its ID, origin port, destination port, deadline, volume, late penalty rate, and required hub capacity class.
- **hubs.csv**: For each hub and each capacity class, the capacity and handling cost per container volume.

## Task

Construct a mathematical optimization model using the provided data. The model should be formulated as a mixed-integer linear program. Do not write code or solve the model.

The following structured data files are provided:

### instance.json
```json
{
  "num_ports": 4,
  "num_hubs": 2,
  "num_services": 3,
  "num_containers": 4,
  "num_hubcap_classes": 2
}
```

### data/services.csv
```csv
service_id,service_cost,service_capacity
S1,1000,500
S2,1500,800
S3,1200,600

```

### data/service_arcs.csv
```csv
service_id,arc_id,origin_node,destination_node,transit_time
S1,1,P1,H1,2
S1,2,H1,P2,3
S2,1,P3,H2,1
S2,2,H2,P4,4
S3,1,P1,H2,2
S3,2,H2,P3,3

```

### data/containers.csv
```csv
container_id,origin_port,destination_port,deadline,volume,late_penalty_rate,hub_class
C1,P1,P2,5,100,10,1
C2,P3,P4,6,200,15,2
C3,P1,P3,4,150,12,1
C4,P2,P4,7,120,8,2

```

### data/hubs.csv
```csv
hub_id,hub_class,capacity,handling_cost
H1,1,300,5
H1,2,200,6
H2,1,250,4
```

An investment firm wants to select a portfolio of financial assets to maximize the total expected return. Each asset has a cost per unit, an expected return per unit, and a risk score. The firm has a total budget that cannot be exceeded. Additionally, the total risk of the portfolio must not exceed a given risk limit. For each asset, there is a maximum number of units that can be held (exposure cap). The decision variables are the continuous units of each asset to purchase. The instance data are provided in data.json.

The following structured data files are provided:

### data.json
```json
{
  "budget": 10000,
  "cost": [
    500,
    800,
    1200,
    600,
    900
  ],
  "expected_return": [
    0.08,
    0.12,
    0.15,
    0.1,
    0.09
  ],
  "risk_score": [
    0.3,
    0.5,
    0.7,
    0.4,
    0.6
  ],
  "risk_limit": 50,
  "exposure_cap": [
    10,
    8,
    5,
    12,
    7
  ]
}
```

import gurobipy as gp
from gurobipy import GRB
import json

with open("data.json", "r") as f:
    data = json.load(f)

budget = data["budget"]
cost = data["cost"]
expected_return = data["expected_return"]
risk_score = data["risk_score"]
risk_limit = data["risk_limit"]
exposure_cap = data["exposure_cap"]

num_assets = len(cost)
assets = range(num_assets)

model = gp.Model("portfolio_optimization")

x = model.addVars(assets, lb=0, ub=exposure_cap, vtype=GRB.CONTINUOUS, name="x")

model.setObjective(gp.quicksum(expected_return[a] * x[a] for a in assets), sense=GRB.MAXIMIZE)

model.addConstr(gp.quicksum(cost[a] * x[a] for a in assets) <= budget, name="BudgetLimit")

model.addConstr(gp.quicksum(risk_score[a] * x[a] for a in assets) <= risk_limit, name="RiskLimit")

model.optimize()

if model.Status == GRB.OPTIMAL:
    print(f"optimal_value = {model.ObjVal}")
elif model.Status == GRB.INFEASIBLE:
    print("optimal_value = INFEASIBLE")
elif model.Status == GRB.UNBOUNDED:
    print("optimal_value = UNBOUNDED")
else:
    print("optimal_value = ERROR")

problem:
A pharmaceutical company blends three active ingredients to produce two types of liquid medicines. Each medicine type has a specific ingredient composition and yields a certain efficacy. The company has limited weekly supply of each ingredient and must meet minimum production requirements for each medicine type. Maximize total efficacy.

**Sets:**
- MEDICINES: set of liquid medicine types (M1, M2)
- INGREDIENTS: set of active ingredients (I1, I2, I3)

**Parameters:**
- composition_{m,i}: milliliters of ingredient i per unit of medicine m
  - M1: I1=2, I2=3, I3=1
  - M2: I1=1, I2=2, I3=4
- efficacy_m: efficacy units per unit of medicine m
  - M1: 5
  - M2: 7
- supply_i: weekly available milliliters of ingredient i
  - I1: 100
  - I2: 150
  - I3: 200
- min_production_m: minimum units of medicine m to produce
  - M1: 10
  - M2: 5

**Decision Variables:**
- x_m: integer units of medicine m to produce (nonnegative)

**Objective:**
Maximize total efficacy = 5*x_M1 + 7*x_M2

**Constraints:**
- Ingredient supply (for each i): sum_{m} composition_{m,i} * x_m <= supply_i
  - I1: 2*x_M1 + 1*x_M2 <= 100
  - I2: 3*x_M1 + 2*x_M2 <= 150
  - I3: 1*x_M1 + 4*x_M2 <= 200
- Minimum production (for each m): x_m >= min_production_m
  - M1: x_M1 >= 10
  - M2: x_M2 >= 5
answer:

The problem involves maximizing total efficacy from producing two liquid medicines (M1, M2) using three active ingredients (I1, I2, I3). Each medicine has a known composition (ml of ingredient per unit), efficacy per unit, and a minimum production requirement. The ingredients have limited weekly supply. Decision variables are integer units of each medicine to produce. The objective is to maximize total efficacy, subject to ingredient supply constraints and minimum production constraints.
</think>

- INGREDIENTS = {I1, I2, I3}

  - M1: I1=2, I2=3, I3=1
  - M2: I1=1, I2=2, I3=4
- efficacy_m: efficacy units per unit of medicine m
  - M1: 5, M2: 7
- supply_i: weekly available milliliters of ingredient i
  - I1: 100, I2: 150, I3: 200
- min_production_m: minimum units of medicine m to produce
  - M1: 10, M2: 5

**Decision Variables:**
- x_m: integer units of medicine m to produce (x_m >= 0)

**Objective:**
Maximize total efficacy = sum_{m in MEDICINES} efficacy_m * x_m = 5*x_M1 + 7*x_M2

**Constraints:**
1. Ingredient supply (for each i in INGREDIENTS):
   sum_{m in MEDICINES} composition_{m,i} * x_m <= supply_i
   - I1: 2*x_M1 + 1*x_M2 <= 100
   - I2: 3*x_M1 + 2*x_M2 <= 150
   - I3: 1*x_M1 + 4*x_M2 <= 200
2. Minimum production (for each m in MEDICINES):
   x_m >= min_production_m
   - M1: x_M1 >= 10
   - M2: x_M2 >= 5
</model>

<python>
import gurobipy as gp
from gurobipy import GRB

# Create model
model = gp.Model("pharmaceutical_blending")

# Sets
medicines = ["M1", "M2"]
ingredients = ["I1", "I2", "I3"]

# Parameters
composition = {
    ("M1", "I1"): 2, ("M1", "I2"): 3, ("M1", "I3"): 1,
    ("M2", "I1"): 1, ("M2", "I2"): 2, ("M2", "I3"): 4
}
efficacy = {"M1": 5, "M2": 7}
supply = {"I1": 100, "I2": 150, "I3": 200}
min_production = {"M1": 10, "M2": 5}

# Decision variables
x = model.addVars(medicines, vtype=GRB.INTEGER, name="x")

# Objective: maximize total efficacy
model.setObjective(gp.quicksum(efficacy[m] * x[m] for m in medicines), GRB.MAXIMIZE)

# Constraints: ingredient supply
for i in ingredients:
    model.addConstr(
        gp.quicksum(composition[m, i] * x[m] for m in medicines) <= supply[i],
        name=f"ingredient_supply_{i}"
    )

# Constraints: minimum production
for m in medicines:
    model.addConstr(x[m] >= min_production[m], name=f"min_production_{m}")

# Optimize
model.optimize()

# Print objective value if optimal
if model.status == GRB.OPTIMAL:
    print(f"Optimal total efficacy: {model.objVal}")
else:
    print("No optimal solution found.")
</python>

problem:
A school district is selecting educational programs for its elementary schools. Each program type consumes a known amount of teacher hours per week and yields a certain number of student learning points. The district has a limited total teacher hours per week, must include at least a minimum number of sessions of a core program, and cannot exceed a maximum ratio of elective to total programs. Determine the number of sessions of each program type to maximize total learning points.

There are three program types: Math, Science, and Art. Each Math session requires 3 teacher hours per week and yields 30 learning points. Each Science session requires 2 teacher hours per week and yields 20 learning points. Each Art session requires 4 teacher hours per week and yields 50 learning points. The district has a total of 180 teacher hours available per week. At least 10 Math sessions must be scheduled. Additionally, the number of Art sessions cannot exceed 40% of the total number of sessions (Math, Science, and Art combined).

Define the decision variables:
- M: number of Math sessions (nonnegative integer)
- S: number of Science sessions (nonnegative integer)
- A: number of Art sessions (nonnegative integer)

Formulate the objective and constraints to maximize the total learning points.
answer:

Constraints: teacher hours capacity (3*M + 2*S + 4*A <= 180), minimum Math sessions (M >= 10), and Art ratio limit (A <= 0.4*(M+S+A)). All variables are nonnegative integers. We implement this in Gurobi 12.x.
</think>

**Sets**
None (individual variables).

**Parameters**
- Math: 3 hours/session, 30 points/session
- Science: 2 hours/session, 20 points/session
- Art: 4 hours/session, 50 points/session
- Total hours available: 180
- Minimum Math sessions: 10
- Maximum Art ratio: 0.4

- S ∈ ℤ₊ (number of Science sessions)

**Objective**
Maximize 30 M + 20 S + 50 A

**Constraints**
1. Teacher hours: 3 M + 2 S + 4 A ≤ 180
2. Minimum Math: M ≥ 10
3. Art ratio: A ≤ 0.4 (M + S + A)  →  A ≤ 0.4 M + 0.4 S + 0.4 A  →  0.6 A ≤ 0.4 M + 0.4 S  →  3 A ≤ 2 M + 2 S  (multiply by 5)  →  equivalently: 2 M + 2 S - 3 A ≥ 0
</model>

<python>
import gurobipy as gp
from gurobipy import GRB

# Create model
model = gp.Model("school_programs")

# Decision variables
M = model.addVar(vtype=GRB.INTEGER, lb=0, name="M")
S = model.addVar(vtype=GRB.INTEGER, lb=0, name="S")
A = model.addVar(vtype=GRB.INTEGER, lb=0, name="A")

# Objective: maximize total learning points
model.setObjective(30 * M + 20 * S + 50 * A, GRB.MAXIMIZE)

# Constraints
model.addConstr(3 * M + 2 * S + 4 * A <= 180, name="teacher_hours_capacity")
model.addConstr(M >= 10, name="min_math")
model.addConstr(A <= 0.4 * (M + S + A), name="art_ratio_limit")

# Optimize
model.optimize()

# Print objective value if optimal
if model.status == GRB.OPTIMAL:
    print(f"Optimal total learning points: {model.objVal}")
else:
    print("No optimal solution found.")

problem:
### Problem Statement

A ceramic tile factory blends raw clays to produce tile batches. Each clay has a cost per ton, a maximum available quantity, and contributes specific chemical/physical properties to the final batch. The factory must produce a batch that meets minimum strength, maximum water absorption, and minimum firing shrinkage requirements. The objective is to minimize the total cost of raw clays used.

### Background

Tile manufacturing requires precise control of material properties. The available clays differ in their cost, availability, and property contributions. The factory needs to determine the optimal blend that satisfies all quality constraints at the lowest material cost.

### Problem Description

Given a set of clay types, each with known cost, availability, and property coefficients, determine the amount of each clay to use (in tons) such that:
- The total amount of each clay does not exceed its availability.
- The weighted sum of strength coefficients meets or exceeds the minimum strength requirement.
- The weighted sum of absorption coefficients does not exceed the maximum absorption limit.
- The weighted sum of shrinkage coefficients meets or exceeds the minimum shrinkage requirement.

### Parameters

All parameters are provided in the file `data.json`. The file contains:
- `Clays`: list of clay type names (strings).
- `Properties`: list of property names (strings).
- `cost`: array of cost per ton for each clay (indexed by Clays).
- `availability`: array of maximum tons available for each clay.
- `composition`: 2D array of property content per ton of clay (rows: Clays, columns: Properties).
- `min_strength`: scalar minimum required strength index.
- `max_absorption`: scalar maximum allowed water absorption percent.
- `min_shrinkage`: scalar minimum required firing shrinkage percent.
- `strength_coeff`: array of strength contribution per ton of each clay.
- `absorption_coeff`: array of absorption contribution per ton of each clay.
- `shrinkage_coeff`: array of shrinkage contribution per ton of each clay.

### Task

Formulate the optimization model using the provided data. Construct a mathematical model that minimizes total cost subject to the constraints described above.

The following structured data files are provided:

### data.json
```json
{
  "Clays": [
    "Clay_A",
    "Clay_B",
    "Clay_C"
  ],
  "Properties": [
    "Strength",
    "Absorption",
    "Shrinkage"
  ],
  "cost": [
    120,
    95,
    150
  ],
  "availability": [
    40,
    30,
    25
  ],
  "composition": [
    [
      0.8,
      0.1,
      0.05
    ],
    [
      0.6,
      0.2,
      0.08
    ],
    [
      0.9,
      0.05,
      0.12
    ]
  ],
  "min_strength": 25,
  "max_absorption": 6,
  "min_shrinkage": 3,
  "strength_coeff": [
    0.8,
    0.6,
    0.9
  ],
  "absorption_coeff": [
    0.1,
    0.2,
    0.05
  ],
  "shrinkage_coeff": [
    0.05,
    0.08,
    0.12
  ]
}
```
answer:
import gurobipy as gp
from gurobipy import GRB
import json

with open('data.json', 'r') as f:
    data = json.load(f)

clays = data['Clays']
cost = data['cost']
availability = data['availability']
strength_coeff = data['strength_coeff']
absorption_coeff = data['absorption_coeff']
shrinkage_coeff = data['shrinkage_coeff']
min_strength = data['min_strength']
max_absorption = data['max_absorption']
min_shrinkage = data['min_shrinkage']

model = gp.Model('ceramic_tile_blending')

x = model.addVars(clays, lb=0, name='x')

model.setObjective(gp.quicksum(cost[i] * x[clay] for i, clay in enumerate(clays)), GRB.MINIMIZE)

for i, clay in enumerate(clays):
    model.addConstr(x[clay] <= availability[i], name=f'availability_{clay}')

model.addConstr(gp.quicksum(strength_coeff[i] * x[clay] for i, clay in enumerate(clays)) >= min_strength, name='strength')
model.addConstr(gp.quicksum(absorption_coeff[i] * x[clay] for i, clay in enumerate(clays)) <= max_absorption, name='absorption')
model.addConstr(gp.quicksum(shrinkage_coeff[i] * x[clay] for i, clay in enumerate(clays)) >= min_shrinkage, name='shrinkage')

model.write('model.lp')

problem:
### Problem Statement

A logistics company blends different fuel additives to produce a final fuel product. The objective is to maximize the total octane rating of the blend while satisfying constraints on sulfur content, total volume, budget, and additive availability.

### Background

Fuel additives have varying octane ratings, sulfur contents, costs, and available quantities. The company must decide how many units of each additive to use in the blend.

### Problem Description

Given a set of fuel additives, each with known octane rating, sulfur content, cost per unit, and maximum available units, determine the number of units of each additive to blend such that:
- The total sulfur content of the blend does not exceed a specified limit.
- The total volume of the blend meets a minimum demand.
- The total cost of purchased additives does not exceed a given budget.
- The usage of each additive does not exceed its availability.

The objective is to maximize the total octane rating of the blend.

### Parameters

All data required for this problem are provided in the file `data.json`. The file contains:
- `I`: list of additive names (strings)
- `octane`: list of floats (octane rating per unit of each additive)
- `sulfur`: list of floats (sulfur content per unit of each additive)
- `cost`: list of floats (cost per unit of each additive)
- `availability`: list of integers (maximum available units of each additive)
- `max_sulfur`: float (maximum allowed sulfur content in the blend)
- `min_demand`: integer (minimum total volume of blend required)
- `budget`: float (total budget for purchasing additives)

### Task

Formulate the optimization model using the provided data. Construct a mathematical model that includes decision variables, an objective function, and all constraints. Do not solve the model.

The following structured data files are provided:

### data.json
```json
{
  "I": [
    "AdditiveA",
    "AdditiveB",
    "AdditiveC"
  ],
  "octane": [
    95.0,
    88.0,
    92.0
  ],
  "sulfur": [
    0.5,
    0.3,
    0.4
  ],
  "cost": [
    2.0,
    1.5,
    1.8
  ],
  "availability": [
    100,
    150,
    120
  ],
  "max_sulfur": 50.0,
  "min_demand": 200,
  "budget": 400.0
}
```
answer:
import gurobipy as gp
from gurobipy import GRB
import json

with open('data.json', 'r') as f:
    data = json.load(f)

I = data['I']
octane = data['octane']
sulfur = data['sulfur']
cost = data['cost']
availability = data['availability']
max_sulfur = data['max_sulfur']
min_demand = data['min_demand']
budget = data['budget']

model = gp.Model('fuel_blend')

x = model.addVars(len(I), vtype=GRB.INTEGER, lb=0, name='x')

model.setObjective(gp.quicksum(octane[i] * x[i] for i in range(len(I))), GRB.MAXIMIZE)

model.addConstr(gp.quicksum(sulfur[i] * x[i] for i in range(len(I))) <= max_sulfur, name='sulfur_limit')
model.addConstr(gp.quicksum(x[i] for i in range(len(I))) >= min_demand, name='demand_requirement')
model.addConstr(gp.quicksum(cost[i] * x[i] for i in range(len(I))) <= budget, name='budget_constraint')

for i in range(len(I)):
    model.addConstr(x[i] <= availability[i], name=f'availability_{I[i]}')

model.write('model.lp')

problem:
## Problem Statement
A hospital network must assign nurses to shifts and units over a planning horizon. The objective is to minimize total assignment cost plus penalty for uncovered slots.

## Background
Each nurse has a contract type that defines minimum and maximum total shifts, maximum hours, maximum overtime, and maximum consecutive working days. Nurses have skill compatibility with units and shift compatibility with shift types. Each nurse may request specific days off. Coverage requirements are given for each unit-slot combination.

## Problem Description
Given the sets of nurses, units, shifts, slots, days, contract types, and skills, formulate a mixed-integer programming model that:
- Assigns nurses to units and shifts (binary decision).
- Tracks overtime hours per nurse per shift (continuous).
- Tracks uncovered slots per unit-slot (continuous).
- Tracks whether a nurse works on a given day (binary).
- Tracks total overtime per nurse (continuous).
- Minimizes total assignment cost plus penalty for uncovered slots.

## Parameters
All concrete data are provided in the file `instance.json` and the CSV files listed below. The meaning of each parameter is as follows:
- `assign_cost[n,u,s]`: Cost of assigning nurse n to unit u on shift s.
- `penalty[u,t]`: Penalty per uncovered slot t in unit u.
- `demand[u,t]`: Required number of nurses for slot t in unit u.
- `max_shifts[n]`: Maximum number of shifts for nurse n.
- `max_hours[n]`: Maximum total hours (including overtime) for nurse n.
- `hours_per_shift[s]`: Hours per shift type s.
- `overtime_cost[n,s]`: Overtime cost per hour for nurse n on shift s.
- `max_overtime[n]`: Maximum overtime hours for nurse n.
- `skill_compat[n,u]`: 1 if nurse n has skill for unit u, else 0.
- `shift_compat[n,s]`: 1 if nurse n can work shift s, else 0.
- `contract_type[n]`: Contract type index for nurse n.
- `min_shifts_per_contract[c]`: Minimum shifts for contract type c.
- `max_shifts_per_contract[c]`: Maximum shifts for contract type c.
- `day_off_req[n,d]`: 1 if nurse n requires day off on day d, else 0.
- `max_consecutive_shifts[n]`: Maximum consecutive working days for nurse n.
- `slot_shift[t,s]`: 1 if slot t corresponds to shift s, else 0.
- `slot_unit[t,u]`: 1 if slot t belongs to unit u, else 0.
- `shift_day[s,d]`: 1 if shift s occurs on day d, else 0.

## Task
Formulate the optimization model using the provided data. The model should include the following decision variables and constraints:

### Decision Variables
- `x[n,u,s]`: binary, 1 if nurse n assigned to unit u on shift s.
- `y[n,s]`: continuous, overtime hours for nurse n on shift s.
- `z[u,t]`: continuous, number of uncovered slots for unit u slot t.
- `w[n,d]`: binary, 1 if nurse n works on day d.
- `v[n]`: continuous, total overtime hours for nurse n.

### Constraints
1. **Coverage**: For each unit u and slot t, sum of assignments plus uncovered slots >= demand.
2. **Skill Compatibility**: For each nurse n, unit u, shift s, assignment <= skill_compat.
3. **Shift Compatibility**: For each nurse n, shift s, total assignments <= shift_compat.
4. **Max Shifts**: For each nurse n, total assignments <= max_shifts.
5. **Max Hours**: For each nurse n, total hours (including overtime) <= max_hours.
6. **Overtime Link**: For each nurse n, total overtime equals sum of overtime hours.
7. **Overtime Bound**: For each nurse n and shift s, overtime hours <= max_overtime * (sum of assignments on that shift).
8. **Contract Min/Max**: For each contract type c, total assignments by nurses of that contract type between min_shifts_per_contract and max_shifts_per_contract.
9. **Day Off**: For each nurse n and day d, if day_off_req=1 then no assignment on that day.
10. **Consecutive Shifts**: For each nurse n and day d, sum of w over consecutive days <= max_consecutive_shifts.
11. **Work Day Link**: For each nurse n and day d, w >= sum of assignments on that day.
12. **Slot-Shift-Unit Consistency**: For each slot t, shift s, unit u, slot_shift * slot_unit <= 1 (auxiliary).

A marketing firm is planning promotional campaigns across four customer segments: Young, Adult, Senior, and Premium. Each segment has a fixed launch cost if a campaign is conducted, a variable cost per impression, a return per impression, and a maximum number of impressions that can be allocated. The firm has a total budget of $80,000 and must achieve at least 5,000 total impressions. The objective is to maximize net profit, defined as total return from impressions minus total fixed and variable costs.

| Segment | Return | FixedCost | VarCost | MaxImpressions |
|---------|--------|-----------|---------|----------------|
| Young   | 12     | 5000      | 2       | 3000           |
| Adult   | 15     | 6000      | 3       | 2500           |
| Senior  | 10     | 4000      | 1       | 2000           |
| Premium | 20     | 8000      | 4       | 1500           |

Formulate an optimization model to determine which campaigns to launch and how many impressions to allocate to each segment, respecting the budget and minimum impression requirements.
answer:
<think>
We have four customer segments, each with a fixed launch cost, variable cost per impression, return per impression, and maximum impressions. We need to decide which campaigns to launch (binary variables) and how many impressions to allocate (continuous variables) to maximize net profit, subject to a total budget of $80,000 and a minimum of 5,000 total impressions.
</think>

<model>
**Sets**
- $S$: set of customer segments = {Young, Adult, Senior, Premium}

**Parameters**
- $Return_s$: return per impression in segment $s$
- $FixedCost_s$: fixed cost if campaign launched in segment $s$
- $VarCost_s$: variable cost per impression in segment $s$
- $MaxImpressions_s$: maximum impressions allowed in segment $s$
- $Budget = 80000$: total budget
- $MinImpressions = 5000$: minimum total impressions required

**Decision Variables**
- $y_s \in \{0,1\}$: 1 if campaign launched in segment $s$, else 0
- $x_s \ge 0$: impressions allocated to segment $s$

**Objective**
Maximize net profit:
$$\sum_{s \in S} Return_s \cdot x_s - \sum_{s \in S} (FixedCost_s \cdot y_s + VarCost_s \cdot x_s)$$

**Constraints**
1. Budget: $\sum_{s \in S} (FixedCost_s \cdot y_s + VarCost_s \cdot x_s) \le Budget$
2. Minimum impressions: $\sum_{s \in S} x_s \ge MinImpressions$
3. Maximum impressions per segment: $x_s \le MaxImpressions_s \cdot y_s, \quad \forall s \in S$
</model>

<python>
import gurobipy as gp
from gurobipy import GRB

# Data
segments = ['Young', 'Adult', 'Senior', 'Premium']
Return = {'Young': 12, 'Adult': 15, 'Senior': 10, 'Premium': 20}
FixedCost = {'Young': 5000, 'Adult': 6000, 'Senior': 4000, 'Premium': 8000}
VarCost = {'Young': 2, 'Adult': 3, 'Senior': 1, 'Premium': 4}
MaxImpressions = {'Young': 3000, 'Adult': 2500, 'Senior': 2000, 'Premium': 1500}
Budget = 80000
MinImpressions = 5000

# Model
model = gp.Model('CampaignSelection')

# Decision variables
y = model.addVars(segments, vtype=GRB.BINARY, name='y')
x = model.addVars(segments, lb=0, vtype=GRB.CONTINUOUS, name='x')

# Objective: maximize net profit
obj = gp.quicksum(Return[s] * x[s] for s in segments) - gp.quicksum(FixedCost[s] * y[s] + VarCost[s] * x[s] for s in segments)
model.setObjective(obj, GRB.MAXIMIZE)

# Budget constraint
model.addConstr(gp.quicksum(FixedCost[s] * y[s] + VarCost[s] * x[s] for s in segments) <= Budget, name='BudgetConstraint')

# Minimum impressions constraint
model.addConstr(gp.quicksum(x[s] for s in segments) >= MinImpressions, name='MinImpressionsConstraint')

# Maximum impressions per segment
for s in segments:
    model.addConstr(x[s] <= MaxImpressions[s] * y[s], name=f'MaxImpressionsConstraint_{s}')

# Optimize
model.optimize()

if model.Status == GRB.OPTIMAL:
    print(f'Optimal net profit: {model.ObjVal}')
</python>

problem:
A financial firm allocates a marketing budget across three investment products: stocks, bonds, and funds. Each product campaign has a known cost and yields a certain number of new accounts. The firm must decide how many campaigns to run for each product to maximize the total number of new accounts, subject to a total budget, minimum campaign requirements for each product, and a product mix constraint that the number of funds campaigns must be at least a given ratio of the number of stocks campaigns.

Data:
- Total budget: $120,000
- Cost per campaign: stocks $15,000, bonds $10,000, funds $20,000
- New accounts per campaign: stocks 300, bonds 200, funds 500
- Minimum campaigns: stocks 2, bonds 1, funds 1
- Minimum ratio of funds campaigns to stocks campaigns: 惯0.5
answer:
<think>
The problem involves deciding how many campaigns to run for each of three investment products (stocks, bonds, funds) to maximize total new accounts, subject to a budget, minimum campaign requirements, and a ratio constraint between funds and stocks campaigns. The decision variables are integer numbers of campaigns. The objective is linear, and all constraints are linear. We will use Gurobi to solve this integer programming problem.
</think>

<model>
**Sets**
- \(P\): set of products = {stocks, bonds, funds}

**Parameters**
- \(\text{cost}_p\): cost per campaign for product \(p\) (stocks: 15000, bonds: 10000, funds: 20000)
- \(\text{accounts}_p\): new accounts per campaign for product \(p\) (stocks: 300, bonds: 200, funds: 500)
- \(\text{budget}\): total budget = 120000
- \(\text{min}_p\): minimum campaigns for product \(p\) (stocks: 2, bonds: 1, funds: 1)
- \(\text{ratio}\): minimum ratio of funds campaigns to stocks campaigns = 0.5

**Decision Variables**
- \(x_p \in \mathbb{Z}_{\ge 0}\): number of campaigns for product \(p\)

**Objective**
Maximize total new accounts:
\[
\sum_{p \in P} \text{accounts}_p \cdot x_p
\]

**Constraints**
1. Budget constraint:
\[
\sum_{p \in P} \text{cost}_p \cdot x_p \le \text{budget}
\]
2. Minimum campaign constraints:
\[
x_p \ge \text{min}_p \quad \forall p \in P
\]
3. Ratio constraint:
\[
x_{\text{funds}} \ge \text{ratio} \cdot x_{\text{stocks}}
\]
</model>

<python>
import gurobipy as gp
from gurobipy import GRB

# Create model
model = gp.Model("marketing_campaigns")

# Data
products = ["stocks", "bonds", "funds"]
cost = {"stocks": 15000, "bonds": 10000, "funds": 20000}
accounts = {"stocks": 300, "bonds": 200, "funds": 500}
min_campaigns = {"stocks": 2, "bonds": 1, "funds": 1}
total_budget = 120000
ratio = 0.5

# Decision variables
x = model.addVars(products, vtype=GRB.INTEGER, name="x")

# Objective: maximize total new accounts
model.setObjective(gp.quicksum(accounts[p] * x[p] for p in products), GRB.MAXIMIZE)

# Budget constraint
model.addConstr(gp.quicksum(cost[p] * x[p] for p in products) <= total_budget, name="budget")

# Minimum campaign constraints
for p in products:
    model.addConstr(x[p] >= min_campaigns[p], name=f"min_{p}")

# Ratio constraint: funds >= 0.5 * stocks
model.addConstr(x["funds"] >= ratio * x["stocks"], name="ratio_funds_stocks")

# Optimize
model.optimize()

# Print objective value
if model.status == GRB.OPTIMAL:
    print(f"Optimal total new accounts: {model.objVal}")
else:
    print("No optimal solution found.")
</python>

problem:
A workshop produces two types of furniture: chairs and tables. Each unit requires wood and labor. The workshop has limited wood and labor per week. Profit per unit is known. The goal is to maximize total weekly profit.

| Product | Profit |
|---------|--------|
| Chairs  | 40     |
| Tables  | 60     |

| Resource | Available |
|----------|-----------|
| Wood     | 120       |
| Labor    | 80        |

| Resource | Chairs | Tables |
|----------|--------|--------|
| Wood     | 2      | 4      |
| Labor    | 3      | 2      |
answer:
<think>
We need to maximize total weekly profit from producing chairs and tables, subject to limited wood and labor. The data is given in markdown tables. We define continuous decision variables for the number of chairs and tables, set the objective as the sum of profit per unit times quantity, and add resource constraints ensuring consumption does not exceed availability.
</think>

<model>
**Sets**
- Products: {Chairs, Tables}
- Resources: {Wood, Labor}

**Parameters**
- profit[p]: profit per unit of product p (Chairs: 40, Tables: 60)
- consumption[r, p]: amount of resource r required per unit of product p (Wood: Chairs 2, Tables 4; Labor: Chairs 3, Tables 2)
- available[r]: weekly available amount of resource r (Wood: 120, Labor: 80)

**Decision Variables**
- x[p] >= 0, continuous: number of units of product p to produce per week

**Objective**
Maximize total profit: sum_{p in Products} profit[p] * x[p]

**Constraints**
For each resource r in Resources:
  sum_{p in Products} consumption[r, p] * x[p] <= available[r]
</model>

<python>
import gurobipy as gp
from gurobipy import GRB

# Create model
model = gp.Model("furniture_production")

# Data
products = ["Chairs", "Tables"]
resources = ["Wood", "Labor"]

profit = {"Chairs": 40, "Tables": 60}
consumption = {
    ("Wood", "Chairs"): 2,
    ("Wood", "Tables"): 4,
    ("Labor", "Chairs"): 3,
    ("Labor", "Tables"): 2
}
available = {"Wood": 120, "Labor": 80}

# Decision variables
x = model.addVars(products, name="x", lb=0, vtype=GRB.CONTINUOUS)

# Objective: maximize total profit
model.setObjective(gp.quicksum(profit[p] * x[p] for p in products), GRB.MAXIMIZE)

# Resource constraints
for r in resources:
    model.addConstr(
        gp.quicksum(consumption[(r, p)] * x[p] for p in products) <= available[r],
        name=f"resource_limit_{r}"
    )

# Optimize
model.optimize()

# Print objective value if optimal
if model.status == GRB.OPTIMAL:
    print(f"Optimal total profit: {model.objVal}")
</python>

problem:
A gas utility operates three compressor stations and two storage facilities to supply gas to four distribution hubs. Each station has a compression capacity and a per-unit profit. Each hub has a gas demand that must be met. Determine the gas flows that maximize total profit.

Data:
- Stations: Station A, Station B, Station C
- Hubs: Hub 1, Hub 2, Hub 3, Hub 4
- Profit per unit (dollars per MMBtu): Station A: 5, Station B: 4, Station C: 6
- Capacity (MMBtu): Station A: 100, Station B: 80, Station C: 120
- Demand (MMBtu): Hub 1: 40, Hub 2: 50, Hub 3: 60, Hub 4: 30

Decision variables: flow[i,j] = gas flow (MMBtu) from station i to hub j, continuous and nonnegative.

Objective: Maximize total profit = 5*(flow_A1+flow_A2+flow_A3+flow_A4) + 4*(flow_B1+flow_B2+flow_B3+flow_B4) + 6*(flow_C1+flow_C2+flow_C3+flow_C4)

Constraints:
- For each station i: sum over hubs j of flow[i,j] ≤ capacity[i]
- For each hub j: sum over stations i of flow[i,j] ≥ demand[j]
answer:
<think>
The problem involves maximizing total profit from gas flows between three compressor stations and four distribution hubs. Each station has a capacity and a per-unit profit, and each hub has a demand that must be met. The decision variables are continuous flows from stations to hubs. The objective is to maximize total profit, subject to station capacity constraints and hub demand constraints.
</think>

<model>
**Sets**
- STATIONS = {Station A, Station B, Station C}
- HUBS = {Hub 1, Hub 2, Hub 3, Hub 4}

**Parameters**
- profit[i]: profit per unit (dollars per MMBtu) from station i
- capacity[i]: maximum gas output (MMBtu) of station i
- demand[j]: gas demand (MMBtu) of hub j

**Decision Variables**
- flow[i,j] >= 0: gas flow (MMBtu) from station i to hub j, continuous

**Objective**
Maximize total profit = sum_{i in STATIONS, j in HUBS} profit[i] * flow[i,j]

**Constraints**
- For each station i: sum_{j in HUBS} flow[i,j] <= capacity[i]
- For each hub j: sum_{i in STATIONS} flow[i,j] >= demand[j]
</model>

<python>
import gurobipy as gp
from gurobipy import GRB

# Data
stations = ['Station A', 'Station B', 'Station C']
hubs = ['Hub 1', 'Hub 2', 'Hub 3', 'Hub 4']
profit = {'Station A': 5, 'Station B': 4, 'Station C': 6}
capacity = {'Station A': 100, 'Station B': 80, 'Station C': 120}
demand = {'Hub 1': 40, 'Hub 2': 50, 'Hub 3': 60, 'Hub 4': 30}

# Model
model = gp.Model('gas_flow_optimization')

# Decision variables
flow = model.addVars(stations, hubs, vtype=GRB.CONTINUOUS, lb=0, name='flow')

# Objective: maximize total profit
model.setObjective(gp.quicksum(profit[i] * flow[i, j] for i in stations for j in hubs), GRB.MAXIMIZE)

# Constraints: station capacity
for i in stations:
    model.addConstr(gp.quicksum(flow[i, j] for j in hubs) <= capacity[i], name=f'capacity_{i}')

# Constraints: hub demand
for j in hubs:
    model.addConstr(gp.quicksum(flow[i, j] for i in stations) >= demand[j], name=f'demand_{j}')

# Optimize
model.optimize()

# Print objective value if optimal
if model.status == GRB.OPTIMAL:
    print(f'Optimal total profit: {model.objVal}')
</python>

problem:
### Problem Statement

A logistics firm blends two fuel grades at multiple depots using three base components to maximize total profit. The blending operation must satisfy composition requirements, component availability, depot capacity, and minimum output constraints.

### Background

The firm operates several depots, each capable of blending two distinct fuel grades. Three base components are available at each depot in limited quantities. Each fuel grade requires a specific fraction of each component. The firm must decide how much of each grade to produce at each depot and how much of each component to allocate to each grade.

### Problem Description

Given the profit per unit for each grade at each depot, the required component fractions for each grade, the available amount of each component at each depot, the total blending capacity at each depot, and the minimum required output for each grade at each depot, formulate an optimization model to maximize total profit.

### Parameters

The data for this problem are provided in the file `data.json`. The file contains the following fields:
- `Depots`: list of depot identifiers.
- `Grades`: list of fuel grade identifiers.
- `Components`: list of component identifiers.
- `profit`: a 2D array indexed by depots and grades, giving profit per unit.
- `comp_ratio`: a 2D array indexed by grades and components, giving the required fraction of each component in each grade.
- `comp_avail`: a 2D array indexed by depots and components, giving the available amount of each component at each depot.
- `capacity`: a 1D array indexed by depots, giving the maximum total blend volume at each depot.
- `min_output`: a 2D array indexed by depots and grades, giving the minimum required output for each grade at each depot.

### Task

Construct a mathematical model that maximizes total profit. The model should include decision variables for the amount of each grade produced at each depot (integer) and the amount of each component used for each grade at each depot (integer). Ensure that the blending composition constraints, component availability constraints, depot capacity constraints, and minimum output constraints are satisfied.

The following structured data files are provided:

### data.json

A furniture workshop produces three types of chairs: Rocking, Office, and Dining. Each chair type requires a certain amount of wood (in board-feet) and labor hours per unit, and yields a profit. The workshop has 8000 board-feet of wood and 1200 labor hours available. Determine the production quantities of each chair type to maximize total profit.

| Chair Type | Wood (board-ft) | Labor (hrs) | Profit ($) |
|---|---|---|---|
| Rocking | 15 | 3.2 | 120 |
| Office | 12 | 2.5 | 90 |
| Dining | 18 | 4.0 | 150 |
answer:
<think>
The problem is a classic product mix optimization. We have three chair types (Rocking, Office, Dining) with known wood and labor requirements per unit and profit per unit. The workshop has limited wood (8000 board-ft) and labor (1200 hours). The goal is to maximize total profit by deciding how many chairs of each type to produce. Since production quantities can be fractional, we use continuous decision variables.
</think>

<model>
**Sets**
- \(C\): set of chair types, \(C = \{\text{Rocking}, \text{Office}, \text{Dining}\}\).

**Parameters**
- \(\text{WoodReq}_c\): board-feet of wood required per chair of type \(c\).
- \(\text{LaborReq}_c\): labor hours required per chair of type \(c\).
- \(\text{Profit}_c\): profit per chair of type \(c\) (in $).
- \(\text{TotalWood} = 8000\): total available board-feet of wood.
- \(\text{TotalLabor} = 1200\): total available labor hours.

**Decision Variables**
- \(\text{Quantity}_c \ge 0\): number of chairs of type \(c\) to produce (continuous).

**Objective**
Maximize total profit:
\[
\sum_{c \in C} \text{Profit}_c \cdot \text{Quantity}_c
\]

**Constraints**
1. Wood limit:
   \[
   \sum_{c \in C} \text{WoodReq}_c \cdot \text{Quantity}_c \le \text{TotalWood}
   \]
2. Labor limit:
   \[
   \sum_{c \in C} \text{LaborReq}_c \cdot \text{Quantity}_c \le \text{TotalLabor}
   \]
</model>

<python>
import gurobipy as gp
from gurobipy import GRB

# Data from the problem statement
chair_types = ["Rocking", "Office", "Dining"]
wood_req = {"Rocking": 15, "Office": 12, "Dining": 18}
labor_req = {"Rocking": 3.2, "Office": 2.5, "Dining": 4.0}
profit = {"Rocking": 120, "Office": 90, "Dining": 150}
total_wood = 8000
total_labor = 1200

# Create model
model = gp.Model("Furniture_Production")

# Decision variables
quantity = model.addVars(chair_types, name="Quantity")

# Objective: maximize total profit
model.setObjective(gp.quicksum(profit[c] * quantity[c] for c in chair_types), GRB.MAXIMIZE)

# Wood constraint
model.addConstr(gp.quicksum(wood_req[c] * quantity[c] for c in chair_types) <= total_wood, name="WoodLimit")

# Labor constraint
model.addConstr(gp.quicksum(labor_req[c] * quantity[c] for c in chair_types) <= total_labor, name="LaborLimit")

# Optimize
model.optimize()

# Print objective value if optimal
if model.status == GRB.OPTIMAL:
    print(f"Optimal total profit: ${model.objVal:.2f}")
else:
    print("No optimal solution found.")
</python>

import gurobipy as gp
from gurobipy import GRB

# Data
concentrates = ['Apple', 'Orange', 'Grape']
cost = {'Apple': 2.5, 'Orange': 3.0, 'Grape': 4.0}
sugar = {'Apple': 0.6, 'Orange': 0.4, 'Grape': 0.8}
acidity = {'Apple': 0.2, 'Orange': 0.1, 'Grape': 0.3}
supply = {'Apple': 600, 'Orange': 500, 'Grape': 400}
demand = 1000
min_sugar_ratio = 0.5
max_acidity_ratio = 0.3

# Model
model = gp.Model('juice_blend')

# Decision variables
x = model.addVars(concentrates, lb=0, name='x')

# Objective: minimize total cost
model.setObjective(gp.quicksum(cost[c] * x[c] for c in concentrates), GRB.MINIMIZE)

# Constraint 1: demand fulfillment
model.addConstr(gp.quicksum(x[c] for c in concentrates) >= demand, name='demand')

# Constraint 2: supply limits
for c in concentrates:
    model.addConstr(x[c] <= supply[c], name=f'supply_{c}')

# Constraint 3: minimum sugar ratio
model.addConstr(gp.quicksum(sugar[c] * x[c] for c in concentrates) >= min_sugar_ratio * gp.quicksum(x[c] for c in concentrates), name='min_sugar')

# Constraint 4: maximum acidity ratio
model.addConstr(gp.quicksum(acidity[c] * x[c] for c in concentrates) <= max_acidity_ratio * gp.quicksum(x[c] for c in concentrates), name='max_acidity')

# Optimize
model.optimize()

if model.status == GRB.OPTIMAL:
    print(f'Optimal total cost: ${model.objVal:.2f}')
    for c in concentrates:
        print(f'{c}: {x[c].x:.2f} liters')
</python>

import gurobipy as gp
from gurobipy import GRB
import json

with open('data.json', 'r') as f:
    data = json.load(f)

compounds = data['compounds']
catalysts = data['catalysts']
raw_materials = data['raw_materials']
profit = data['profit']
catalyst_usage = data['catalyst_usage']
catalyst_available = data['catalyst_available']
material_usage = data['material_usage']
material_supply = data['material_supply']
min_production = data['min_production']

model = gp.Model('chemical_plant')

production = model.addVars(compounds, lb=0, vtype=GRB.CONTINUOUS, name='production')

model.setObjective(gp.quicksum(profit[c] * production[c] for c in compounds), GRB.MAXIMIZE)

for k in catalysts:
    model.addConstr(
        gp.quicksum(catalyst_usage[c][k] * production[c] for c in compounds) <= catalyst_available[k],
        name=f'catalyst_limit_{k}'
    )

for m in raw_materials:
    model.addConstr(
        gp.quicksum(material_usage[c][m] * production[c] for c in compounds) <= material_supply[m],
        name=f'material_limit_{m}'
    )

for c in compounds:
    model.addConstr(
        production[c] >= min_production[c],
        name=f'min_production_{c}'
    )

model.write('model.lp')

problem:
A logistics company operates a network of pipelines connecting supply depots to demand hubs. Each pipeline has a maximum flow rate and a cost per unit of flow. The company must determine flow quantities to minimize total transportation cost while respecting supply limits at depots, demand requirements at hubs, and a total budget on transportation cost.

There are three supply depots: Depot A, Depot B, and Depot C. Their supply capacities are 100, 150, and 120 units, respectively.

There are two demand hubs: Hub X and Hub Y. Their demand requirements are 180 and 140 units, respectively.

The cost per unit flow (in dollars) from each depot to each hub is given below:
- Depot A to Hub X: 5
- Depot A to Hub Y: 7
- Depot B to Hub X: 6
- Depot B to Hub Y: 4
- Depot C to Hub X: 8
- Depot C to Hub Y: 3

The maximum flow capacity on each pipeline (in units) is:
- Depot A to Hub X: 80
- Depot A to Hub Y: 60
- Depot B to Hub X: 90
- Depot B to Hub Y: 100
- Depot C to Hub X: 70
- Depot C to Hub Y: 90

The total available budget for transportation cost is $2000.

Formulate an optimization model to minimize the total transportation cost, subject to supply limits, demand fulfillment, pipeline capacities, and the budget constraint.
answer:
<think>
We need to minimize total transportation cost subject to supply limits, demand requirements, pipeline capacities, and a budget constraint. The decision variables are continuous flows from each depot to each hub. The objective is linear, and all constraints are linear.
</think>

<model>
**Sets**
- DEPOTS = {A, B, C}
- HUBS = {X, Y}

**Parameters**
- supply_d: supply capacity at depot d (A:100, B:150, C:120)
- demand_h: demand requirement at hub h (X:180, Y:140)
- cost_{d,h}: cost per unit flow from depot d to hub h
- capacity_{d,h}: maximum flow on pipeline (d,h)
- budget: total available budget = 2000

**Decision Variables**
- flow_{d,h} >= 0, continuous: flow quantity from depot d to hub h

**Objective**
Minimize total cost = sum_{d in DEPOTS, h in HUBS} cost_{d,h} * flow_{d,h}

**Constraints**
1. Supply limit: for each depot d, sum_{h in HUBS} flow_{d,h} <= supply_d
2. Demand fulfillment: for each hub h, sum_{d in DEPOTS} flow_{d,h} >= demand_h
3. Pipeline capacity: for each (d,h), flow_{d,h} <= capacity_{d,h}
4. Budget: sum_{d in DEPOTS, h in HUBS} cost_{d,h} * flow_{d,h} <= budget
</model>

<python>
import gurobipy as gp
from gurobipy import GRB

# Data
depots = ['A', 'B', 'C']
hubs = ['X', 'Y']
supply = {'A': 100, 'B': 150, 'C': 120}
demand = {'X': 180, 'Y': 140}
cost = {('A','X'): 5, ('A','Y'): 7, ('B','X'): 6, ('B','Y'): 4, ('C','X'): 8, ('C','Y'): 3}
capacity = {('A','X'): 80, ('A','Y'): 60, ('B','X'): 90, ('B','Y'): 100, ('C','X'): 70, ('C','Y'): 90}
budget = 2000

# Model
m = gp.Model('transportation')

# Variables
flow = m.addVars(depots, hubs, name='flow')

# Objective
m.setObjective(gp.quicksum(cost[d,h] * flow[d,h] for d in depots for h in hubs), GRB.MINIMIZE)

# Supply constraints
m.addConstrs((flow.sum(d,'*') <= supply[d] for d in depots), name='supply')

# Demand constraints
m.addConstrs((flow.sum('*',h) >= demand[h] for h in hubs), name='demand')

# Pipeline capacity constraints
m.addConstrs((flow[d,h] <= capacity[d,h] for d in depots for h in hubs), name='capacity')

# Budget constraint
m.addConstr(gp.quicksum(cost[d,h] * flow[d,h] for d in depots for h in hubs) <= budget, name='budget')

# Solve
m.optimize()

if m.Status == GRB.OPTIMAL:
    print(f'Optimal total cost: {m.ObjVal}')
</python>

problem:
A cross-dock logistics operator must schedule dock door assignments and trailer movements over a multi-day planning horizon. Each incoming trailer has a type, an arrival day, a latest allowed departure day, a destination zone, and a volume (in pallets). The operator must assign each trailer to exactly one compatible door during one shift, or else delay it within its allowed window. Delays incur a per-day cost, and reassigning a trailer from one day to the next incurs an additional cost. Each door has a daily pallet capacity, a maximum number of trailers it can handle per day, and a maximum number of labor shifts that can be assigned to it per day. Each shift has a start and end time slot, a wage cost, and a door-specific assignment cost. A shift can be assigned to a door on a given day only if at least one trailer is assigned to that door during that shift. For each destination zone and day, there is a required pallet demand; unmet demand is allowed but incurs a penalty per pallet. The objective is to minimize the total handling cost (trailer-to-door assignment), delay cost, reassignment cost, zone penalty, and labor cost (shift wages plus shift-door assignment costs).

A hospital needs to schedule nursing shifts for two departments (emergency and general) over a week (Monday to Sunday). Each emergency shift lasts 8 hours and costs $200; each general shift lasts 6 hours and costs $150. The minimum nursing hours required each day are: Monday 40, Tuesday 36, Wednesday 44, Thursday 48, Friday 52, Saturday 32, Sunday 28. The weekly budget for shift costs is $10,000. The hospital must meet the daily coverage requirement, ensure that the total weekly cost does not exceed the budget, and schedule at least as many emergency shifts as general shifts each day. The objective is to minimize the total number of shifts scheduled (both emergency and general combined). Determine the optimal number of emergency and general shifts to schedule each day.
answer:
<think>
The problem involves scheduling emergency and general nursing shifts over a week (Monday to Sunday). Each emergency shift lasts 8 hours and costs $200; each general shift lasts 6 hours and costs $150. Daily minimum nursing hours are given, and the weekly budget is $10,000. Additionally, each day must have at least as many emergency shifts as general shifts. The objective is to minimize the total number of shifts scheduled. This is a mixed-integer linear programming problem with integer decision variables for the number of each shift type per day.
</think>

<model>
**Sets**
- DAYS = {Monday, Tuesday, Wednesday, Thursday, Friday, Saturday, Sunday}
- SHIFTS = {emergency, general}

**Parameters**
- duration[s]: hours per shift for shift type s (8 for emergency, 6 for general)
- cost[s]: cost per shift for shift type s (200 for emergency, 150 for general)
- min_hours[d]: minimum nursing hours required on day d
- budget: total weekly budget ($10,000)

**Decision Variables**
- x[d,s] ∈ ℤ⁺: number of shifts of type s scheduled on day d

**Objective**
Minimize total number of shifts:
min ∑_{d∈DAYS} ∑_{s∈SHIFTS} x[d,s]

**Constraints**
1. Daily coverage: ∑_{s∈SHIFTS} duration[s] * x[d,s] ≥ min_hours[d]  ∀ d ∈ DAYS
2. Weekly budget: ∑_{d∈DAYS} ∑_{s∈SHIFTS} cost[s] * x[d,s] ≤ budget
3. Emergency precedence: x[d,'emergency'] ≥ x[d,'general']  ∀ d ∈ DAYS
4. Variable non-negativity and integrality: x[d,s] ≥ 0, integer
</model>

<python>
import gurobipy as gp
from gurobipy import GRB

# Create model
model = gp.Model("nursing_shift_scheduling")

# Data
days = ["Monday", "Tuesday", "Wednesday", "Thursday", "Friday", "Saturday", "Sunday"]
shifts = ["emergency", "general"]
duration = {"emergency": 8, "general": 6}
cost = {"emergency": 200, "general": 150}
min_hours = {"Monday": 40, "Tuesday": 36, "Wednesday": 44, "Thursday": 48, "Friday": 52, "Saturday": 32, "Sunday": 28}
budget = 10000

# Decision variables
x = {}
for d in days:
    for s in shifts:
        x[d, s] = model.addVar(vtype=GRB.INTEGER, lb=0, name=f"x_{d}_{s}")

# Objective: minimize total number of shifts
model.setObjective(gp.quicksum(x[d, s] for d in days for s in shifts), GRB.MINIMIZE)

# Constraint 1: daily coverage
for d in days:
    model.addConstr(
        gp.quicksum(duration[s] * x[d, s] for s in shifts) >= min_hours[d],
        name=f"coverage_{d}"
    )

# Constraint 2: weekly budget
model.addConstr(
    gp.quicksum(cost[s] * x[d, s] for d in days for s in shifts) <= budget,
    name="budget_limit"
)

# Constraint 3: emergency shifts >= general shifts each day
for d in days:
    model.addConstr(
        x[d, "emergency"] >= x[d, "general"],
        name=f"emergency_precedence_{d}"
    )

# Optimize
model.optimize()

# Print objective value
if model.status == GRB.OPTIMAL:
    print(f"Optimal total number of shifts: {model.objVal}")
    for d in days:
        for s in shifts:
            print(f"{d} {s}: {int(x[d, s].X)}")
else:
    print("No optimal solution found.")
</python>

problem:
A cross-dock operator must assign incoming pallets to outbound trailers and schedule loading doors. Each pallet has a type and destination; each trailer has a capacity and destination set. The goal is to minimize total handling and penalty costs via binary assignment, trailer activation, door assignment, and pallet rejection decisions. The instance data are provided in the files instance.json, data/pallets.csv, data/trailers.csv, data/doors.csv, data/assignment_costs.csv, data/door_costs.csv, data/pallet_door_penalties.csv, data/destination_requirements.csv, data/type_requirements.csv, and data/yard_capacity.csv. The problem includes the following sets: PALLETS, TRAILERS, DOORS, PALLET_TYPES, DESTINATIONS, YARDS. Decision variables: x[p,t] (binary, 1 if pallet p assigned to trailer t), y[t] (binary, 1 if trailer t activated), z[d,t] (binary, 1 if door d assigned to trailer t), w[p] (binary, 1 if pallet p rejected), u[p,d] (binary, 1 if pallet p assigned to door d). Objective: minimize sum_{p,t} assign_cost[p,t] * x[p,t] + sum_t activate_cost[t] * y[t] + sum_{d,t} door_cost[d,t] * z[d,t] + sum_p reject_penalty[p] * w[p] + sum_{p,d} time_penalty[p,d] * u[p,d]. Constraints: (1) Each pallet assigned to one trailer or rejected: sum_t x[p,t] + w[p] = 1 for all p. (2) Trailer capacity enforced only if activated: sum_p x[p,t] <= trailer_capacity[t] * y[t] for all t. (3) Minimum pallets for trailer activation: sum_p x[p,t] >= min_pallets_per_trailer[t] * y[t] for all t. (4) Maximum pallets per trailer: sum_p x[p,t] <= max_pallets_per_trailer[t] * y[t] for all t. (5) Pallet type must match trailer type: x[p,t] <= 1 if pallet_type[p] == trailer_type[t] else 0 for all p,t. (6) Pallet destination must match trailer destination: x[p,t] <= 1 if pallet_dest[p] == trailer_dest[t] else 0 for all p,t. (7) Door assigned only if trailer activated: z[d,t] <= y[t] for all d,t. (8) Max trailers per door: sum_t z[d,t] <= door_capacity[d] for all d. (9) Pallet-door assignment requires pallet-trailer and door-trailer assignments: u[p,d] <= sum_t (x[p,t] * z[d,t]) for all p,d. (10) Max activated trailers per yard zone: sum_{t: trailer_yard[t]==yd} y[t] <= yard_capacity[yd] for all yd in YARDS. (11) Minimum fraction of pallets served per destination: sum_{p: pallet_dest[p]==dest} (1 - w[p]) >= dest_coverage_min[dest] * count_{p: pallet_dest[p]==dest} for all dest. (12) Minimum fraction of pallets served per type: sum_{p: pallet_type[p]==pt} (1 - w[p]) >= type_coverage_min[pt] * count_{p: pallet_type[p]==pt} for all pt. (13) Pallet-door assignments limited by trailer capacity per door: sum_p u[p,d] <= trailer_capacity[t] * z[d,t] for all d,t.
